title: Preventing Iran’s Military Reconstitution
gist: CSIS assesses that U.S./Israeli strikes in 2026 gutted Iran's conventional navy, ports, and missile/drone production, and that Iran will reconstitute by rerouting dual-use procurement away from the UAE toward Pakistan, the Caspian, and direct Chinese sourcing — opening a narrow window for the U.S. to extend Russia-style 'no reexport' sanctions tools to Iran.

insights.claim: U.S. and Israeli strikes in 2026 destroyed nearly all of Iran's conventional naval surface fleet (the 'gray hull' IRIN and IRGCN navy) and damaged its principal military shipyards, with sunken vessels blocking key port berths.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Iran's near-term naval rebuild and bulk-import capacity are bottlenecked at the ports — a chokepoint for any reconstitution-procurement watch.
insights.quote: Commercial satellite imagery confirms the loss of nearly all of Tehran’s conventional naval surface fleet
insights.locator: What Is Gone: Assessing the Damage

insights.claim: U.S. assessments report Iran retains 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile but only 40 percent of its drone arsenal after firing 6,770 drones and missiles as of April 1, 2026.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The drone gap (vs. missiles) signals where Iran's most urgent replenishment demand — and thus procurement vulnerability — will concentrate.
insights.quote: Tehran retains 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile but only 40 percent of its drone arsenal
insights.locator: What Is Gone: Assessing the Damage

insights.claim: Iran's missile and nuclear infrastructure is largely buried in hardened underground facilities, so open-source imagery cannot verify the true extent of production-capacity damage below ground.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Headline 'damage' figures understate uncertainty — surviving underground capacity could let Iran reconstitute faster than visible imagery implies.
insights.quote: While surface damage is visible, the extent of damage below is publicly unknown.

insights.claim: Leaked documents from Iranian defense firm Sahara Thunder allege every Shahed drone plant has two replacement sites for redundancy, and the drones are intentionally simple to produce.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Built-in redundancy means strikes on visible drone plants likely won't halt Shahed output — drone reconstitution will be hard to stop kinetically.
insights.quote: every drone plant has two replacement sites for redundancy

insights.claim: Iran's four near-term reconstitution priorities are clearing blocked ports, rebuilding weapons/munitions facilities, replenishing the drone fleet, and building up IRGCN asymmetric small-boat and unmanned maritime forces.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Defines the demand basket — salvage gear, machine tools, drone components, marine engines — that procurement-monitoring and export-control targeting should track.

insights.claim: Tehran will lean further into the IRGCN's cheaper, faster-to-build small-boat, fast-attack, and unmanned maritime platforms because its conventional fleet and shipyards are degraded.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Expect Iran's Gulf threat to shift toward asymmetric harassment/denial — relevant to shipping-insurance and Strait-of-Hormuz risk premia.
insights.quote: platforms that are cheaper, faster to build, and better suited to the harassment and denial missions

insights.claim: As of November 2022, Iran was assessed able to produce roughly 5,000 engines and 500 airframes annually for the Shahed-136 drone, but still depends on imported components such as electronics and engines.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Component imports — not finished drones — are the supply-chain pressure point for disrupting Iran's drone output.
insights.quote: approximately 5,000 engines and 500 airframes for the Shahed-136 drone annually

insights.claim: The key gap in allied Iran sanctions is extraterritoriality: U.S. controls follow the item through reexport and via foreign-direct-product rules, while EU and Japanese controls generally stop at the first export border.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The non-extraterritorial reach of EU/Japan controls is the structural loophole Iran will exploit through transshipment.
insights.quote: EU and Japanese controls generally stop at the border of the first export
insights.locator: The Trade Control Landscape: What Iran Is Allowed to Buy

insights.claim: CSIS argues the U.S. should extend the Russia-sanctions template to Iran — chiefly the EU's 'no reexport' contract clause and its anti-circumvention tool, which the EU first used in April 2026 to ban certain machine-tool and telecom sales to Kyrgyzstan.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A 'no reexport to Iran' clause would be the concrete near-term policy lever — watch for EU/US adoption as a leading indicator of tightened Iran controls.
insights.quote: A December 2023 requirement obligates exporters to include a “no reexport to Russia” clause in contracts for certain sensitive goods.
insights.locator: How Iran Will Seek to Procure

insights.claim: In 2023 the EU expanded controls to broadly-defined commercial drone components, CNC machine tools and chemicals, and both the EU and Japan tightened further in September 2025 under the UN snapback sanctions.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Snapback re-imposition materially widened the controlled-goods perimeter — compliance burden and circumvention risk for firms selling dual-use items is rising.
insights.locator: The Trade Control Landscape: What Iran Is Allowed to Buy

insights.claim: 95 percent of the UAE's direct non-oil exports to Iran in 2024 were reexports, making the UAE Iran's top import source despite producing little of what it ships.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The UAE reexport hub is the single largest node in Iran's dual-use supply chain — its disruption reshapes the whole procurement map.
insights.quote: 95 percent of the UAE’s direct, non-oil exports to Iran in 2024 were reexports
insights.locator: How Iran Will Seek to Procure: New Trade Routes

insights.claim: Iran launched over 2,800 missiles and drones at the UAE between February and May 2026 — more than at any other country including Israel — prompting the UAE to close its Tehran embassy and likely forcing Iran to find new reexport routes.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The Iran-UAE rupture is the catalyst severing Iran's main procurement artery — a structural shift, not a temporary disruption.
insights.quote: Iran launched over 2,800 missiles and drones at the UAE between February and May of 2026, more than at any other country—including Israel.
insights.locator: How Iran Will Seek to Procure: New Trade Routes

insights.claim: Pakistan reportedly authorized reexports to Iran through its ports and territory as of April 25, 2026, giving Iran a contiguous land route less exposed to U.S. or Israeli interdiction.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Pakistan transit volumes are the early indicator CSIS flags for how Iran is rebalancing procurement — a monitorable signal.
insights.quote: Pakistan reportedly authorized reexports to Iran through its ports and territory as of April 25.
insights.locator: How Iran Will Seek to Procure: New Trade Routes

insights.claim: Caspian Sea routes through Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan and Turkmenistan are attractive to Iran because the closed sea governed by its five littoral states bypasses Persian Gulf chokepoints and is harder for the U.S. to interdict.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The Caspian is the hardest-to-disrupt reconstitution channel — and the same route Russia may now use to supply Iran with drone components.
insights.quote: The Caspian is a closed sea governed by its five littoral states.
insights.locator: How Iran Will Seek to Procure: New Trade Routes

themes.theme: China as Supplier of First Resort

insights.claim: Across all four of Iran's priority procurement categories, China is the most likely supplier of first resort, with U.S./allied versions preferable but rarely essential.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: China's 'good enough' manufacturing is the backstop that makes Western export controls necessary but insufficient to stop Iran's rebuild.
insights.quote: China is the most likely supplier of first resort—U.S. and allied versions are generally preferable, but rarely essential.

insights.claim: China led global machine-tool production at 36.9 percent in 2025 and global exports at 21.6 percent in early 2026, and has already largely replaced German CNC tools in Iran's imports.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: China's machine-tool dominance gives Iran a sanctions-resilient path to rebuild weapons-production capacity at scale.
insights.quote: China is the global leader in both machine tool production (36.9 percent in 2025) and global exports (21.6 percent in early 2026)

insights.claim: Germany's Siemens and Japan's Fanuc dominate CNC machine-tool controllers — Fanuc on an estimated 50–60 percent of CNC machines worldwide and Siemens on another 20–25 percent — and Chinese CNC machines also rely on these controllers.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The Siemens/Fanuc controller chokepoint is the highest-leverage disruption target — controllers are the 'brain' even Chinese machines cannot easily replace.
insights.quote: Fanuc controllers alone are estimated to be on 50–60 percent of CNC machines worldwide, with Siemens on another 20–25 percent.

insights.claim: Iranian distributor Technoset, a former authorized seller of Siemens, Fanuc, Heidenhain and Mitsubishi, openly advertised 'import of sanctioned goods' on Instagram after the EU reimposed sanctions in December 2025.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Brazen open advertising of sanctions evasion illustrates the enforcement gap that the proposed extraterritorial tools aim to close.
insights.quote: import and clearance of goods from the United States, Germany, and Japan . . . lifting sanctions

insights.claim: Iran's drone program relies on internationally sourced testing and production equipment from China, including dynamometers contracted from China's CAMA (a military-civil fusion firm under AVIC) via the sanctioned Sahara Thunder.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Disrupting Iran's acquisition of foreign drone-production tooling is a leverage point to slow asymmetric-drone reconstitution.

insights.claim: Entities in China are a key source of Western-origin electronics for Iranian drone manufacturers, but both Iranian and Russian drone programs are increasingly using domestic Chinese components, which remain subject to U.S. foreign-direct-product controls.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The shift to domestic Chinese parts narrows Western leverage to the foreign-direct-product rule, raising the diplomatic stakes with Beijing.
insights.quote: both the Iranian and Russian drone programs are increasingly using domestic Chinese components

insights.claim: Iran claims far larger imports of CNC controllers and machine tools from Turkey than Turkey reports exporting, and sizeable trade-data gaps like this can signal concealed procurement via Turkish intermediaries.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Mirror-trade-statistics gaps are a usable analytic tool to flag covert reconstitution flows in near-real time.
insights.quote: Iran claims far more imports of CNC controllers and machine tools from Turkey than Turkey reports sending to Iran.

insights.claim: Since 2022 about 40 percent of Iran's marine outboard motors came from the UAE, 32 percent from China and 10 percent from Turkey, while Iran's gas-turbine imports came almost exclusively from Turkey — much of it likely transshipped from GE Marine, the EU, Japan and the US.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Marine-engine import patterns reveal which intermediary jurisdictions to target as Iran rebuilds its asymmetric naval fleet.

insights.claim: Iran has over 40 years of experience operating worldwide covert acquisition networks, so the same outreach, red-flag indicators and end-user screening built to block diversion to Russia translate directly to the Iran problem.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Reusing the Russia enforcement playbook is the practical path; the overlapping jurisdictions and evasion typologies make it transferable.
insights.quote: Iran has over 40 years of experience in establishing and operating worldwide covert acquisition networks

insights.claim: There is already limited direct allied trade with Iran — the EU exported $4.3 billion in goods to Iran in 2024 and Japan only $89 million — so the central enforcement challenge is circumvention via third-country reexport, not direct sales.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The low direct-trade baseline means policy effort should concentrate on transshipment hubs rather than first-party export bans.
insights.quote: the European Union exported $4.3 billion in goods to Iran in 2024 and Japan exported $89 million
